checks.a_analysis: I reviewed the output JSON against the source PDF slides. All text elements in the output sections correspond to content present in the source. There is no evidence of fabricated body text, invented sections, or hallucinated introductory/concluding remarks.

I verified all URLs and email addresses provided in the output (e.g., ncbroadband.gov/sbp, firsttech@nc.gov, it.nc.gov) against the source PDF. No fabricated or hallucinated contact information was found. The output correctly reflects the contact details it captured.

Each of the 27 slides from the source document is accounted for in the output, either through extracted text or descriptive alt-text for images (e.g., the map on slide 5 and adoption map on slide 11). No entire page or section was omitted.

The tables on slides 3 (Broadband Speeds) and 4 (Typical Broadband Usage) were converted accurately. Slide 3 has all 18 activity rows. Slide 4 has all 4 data rows. The list of grant awards on slide 24, while formatted as a paragraph in the output, contains all entries found in the source.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: On slide 19, 'Community: Technical Assistance Team', the output captures the general description of the team's role but completely omits the regional contact information for the West, Central, and East regions. This includes names, phone numbers, and email addresses for three specific staff members, which constitutes a significant portion of the slide's useful information.
checks.b_evidence_source: West: Keith Conover, 828-450-7933, Keith.Conover@nc.gov; Central: Jim Corrin, 919-353-1126, Jim.Corrin@nc.gov; East: George T. Collier, 919-754-6558, George.Collier@nc.gov
checks.b_evidence_output: The contact details for the regional leads are entirely missing from the element list for this section.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text that was successfully extracted maintains the meaning and terminology of the source. No critical wording changes (like altering numbers or legal requirements) were observed in the captured content.

checks.a_analysis: I have carefully compared the source PDF with the JSON output. Each slide's unique content is represented exactly once in the JSON. There are no instances where a paragraph or list item is repeated within a section or appears in multiple places incorrectly. Standard repeated elements like logos are correctly handled as images.

The heading hierarchy is logical and consistent. The document uses section titles (which act as top-level containers) followed by H2 for sub-headings (e.g., 'Multiple technology types...' on Page 2, 'Executive Order 91' on Page 17) and H3 for smaller subsections (e.g., the grant details on Page 23). There are no skipped levels or illogical nesting.

Tables in the JSON faithfully represent the source material. For example, the 'Broadband Speeds' table on Page 3 correctly uses colspans for category headers ('General Usage', 'Watching Video', etc.) which match the layout in the PDF. The 'Typical Broadband Usage' table on Page 4 preserves the 4-column structure. Other visual layouts, like the social media links on Page 27, have been logically organized into tables for structural clarity.

The JSON follows the logical top-to-bottom, page-by-page flow of the original presentation. Elements within complex slides, such as Page 19 (Technical Assistance Team) and Page 21 (Playbook), are linearized in a way that preserves the intended reading order.

Lists throughout the document (e.g., technology types on Page 2, agencies on Page 18, barriers on Page 12) are correctly identified and represented using the list structure. Nested lists on Page 2 accurately reflect the sub-bullets in the source.

All visible URLs and contact emails in the source PDF have been captured correctly as links in the JSON. This includes the FCC guide links on Pages 3 and 4, the state broadband plan link on Page 16, the contact email on Page 26, and the website link on Page 27.

checks.a_analysis: The visual organization of the converted document matches the source PDF. Content from the slides is presented in a logical, single-column vertical flow that preserves the original slide-by-slide progression. Information is grouped correctly under corresponding headings (e.g., 'Broadband Technology Types', 'Broadband Speeds', 'Typical Broadband Usage'). The interactive elements like the Table of Contents at the beginning are standard accessible HTML features and not layout defects.

The tables on pages 3 and 4 of the PDF ('Broadband Speeds' and 'Typical Broadband Usage') are correctly rendered as HTML tables. They maintain clear row and column associations, and headers are visually distinct. The 'Typical Broadband Usage' table in the HTML correctly represents the complex header structure (Light/Moderate/High Use) and the 4x3 grid of speed values.

Key meaningful images from the PDF are missing or replaced with placeholder green blocks in the HTML rendering. Specifically, the title page icon (antenna), the broadband service inventory map (page 5), the infographic series of houses (pages 6-9), and the Technical Assistance Regions map (page 19) are not visually present in the HTML screenshot, appearing only as green rectangular placeholders. These are meaningful data visualizations (maps and infographics) that are central to the document's content.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 5: North Carolina Broadband Service Inventory map; Page 11: 2017 County Broadband Adoption Rates map; Page 19: Technical Assistance Regions map.
checks.b_evidence_output: Green rectangular blocks where the maps and infographics should be located.
checks.b_evidence_location: Sections: 'North Carolina Broadband Service Inventory', 'Grid of Red Houses...', '2017 County Broadband Adoption Rates', 'Community: Technical Assistance Team'

checks.a_analysis: There is no evidence of duplicated body content. While titles appear in the Table of Contents and then again as section headings in the body, this is an intentional accessibility feature. The actual informational content (paragraphs, list items, table data) appears only once.
